threeFactor.lead1: Your email, your work account — they all stopped trusting passwords alone years ago. A password plus a one-time code, a tap to approve, a fingerprint on a device the system already knows. That's two-factor authentication, and it's why stolen passwords rarely take over accounts anymore.
threeFactor.lead2: Time clocks never got the memo. Most still trust a single factor: a PIN anyone can shout across a stockroom, a QR code anyone can photograph, or a face scan that hauls your staff's biometrics into someone's cloud. Each one is a single point of failure — and buddy punching is what failure looks like on a timesheet.
threeFactor.lead3: DailyBrew brings the same idea to the café: three factors, not one.
threeFactor.factor1: Something you have — the verified device. Within a single day, only one device per employee can clock in, and the check-out has to come from the same device as the check-in. No other employee can use that device that day either. The next day, they can use a different phone — the constraint is per-shift, not per-lifetime — so an upgraded phone or a borrowed device on a fresh day doesn't break anything.
threeFactor.factor2: Somewhere you are — the verified network. Every clock-in records the IP. Punches from outside your shop's network are blocked, or flagged for you to review — so a clock-in "from the parking lot" on mobile data doesn't slip through.
threeFactor.factor3: Something you tap — the NFC tag (beta). A coin-sized sticker by the espresso machine. A tap that has to happen within a few centimeters of a tag that lives only at your shop.
threeFactor.factorsOutro: Device (something you have) + network (somewhere you are) + tag (something you tap). To fake a punch you'd need a co-worker's enrolled phone, physically on your shop's WiFi, tapping a tag that's bolted to your wall. At that point they're just... at work.
threeFactor.noFaceP1: Face recognition was designed for 200-person factories, not four-person cafés. It verifies who's in front of the camera by storing a mathematical model of their face — biometric data that triggers laws like Illinois' BIPA, which has produced settlements in the hundreds of millions. Your barista doesn't want their face in a vendor's database, and you don't want the legal exposure.
threeFactor.noFaceP2: DailyBrew collects zero biometric data. We verify the device, not the person's body.
threeFactor.noGps: Geofencing tells you a phone is near the shop — not whose phone, and not whether they're actually inside. It also demands always-on location permission and drains batteries, which is why staff hate it. We skip GPS entirely. The network check answers "are they on the shop's WiFi?" far more precisely than a GPS bubble, with no battery cost and no location tracking.
threeFactor.h2Forgotten: What about forgotten phones and broken screens?
threeFactor.forgottenIntro: Small teams need flexibility, so the system has honest escape hatches:
threeFactor.forgotten1: Forgot your phone? Your manager clocks you in from the dashboard — logged as an override with their name, the time, and a reason.
threeFactor.forgotten2: New phone? Your manager resets your device binding in two taps; the old phone is deauthorized automatically.
threeFactor.forgotten3: No NFC on an older phone? The QR code is the universal fallback, and it runs through the same device and network checks.
threeFactor.forgottenOutro: Every override is in the audit trail. Flexibility, without a PIN to share.
threeFactor.startP1: QR + device + IP verification is live today and free for up to 10 active employees. NFC tap-to-clock-in is in beta — same verification stack, just a faster gesture.
threeFactor.startCta: See the <linkFeatures>verification features</linkFeatures>, check <linkPricing>pricing</linkPricing>, or <linkSignup>start free</linkSignup> and have honest timesheets running before the morning rush.

buddyPunchingTax.lead1: A 5-person café paying $30,000 a month in wages loses roughly $660 every month to buddy punching. That's not the price of one fancy espresso machine per year — it's two. Over a decade, it pays for the build-out.
buddyPunchingTax.lead2: The 2.2% number comes from Nucleus Research; the American Payroll Association estimates roughly 75% of businesses are affected. On a small team the percentage is the same as on a 200-person operation, but the visibility is worse: one staff member covering for a chronically late friend over six months is the difference between a tight budget and a bad month.
buddyPunchingTax.h2What: What buddy punching actually looks like
buddyPunchingTax.whatIntro: It's almost never theft for theft's sake. It's:
buddyPunchingTax.what1: The texted "I clocked you in" from the parking lot
buddyPunchingTax.what2: The PIN written on the back of the iPad by the register
buddyPunchingTax.what3: The "we share a phone — Mia logs in for both of us" arrangement
buddyPunchingTax.what4: The QR code photographed and shared in a group chat
buddyPunchingTax.whatOutro: Each one is invisible in a normal time-clock report.
buddyPunchingTax.badFace: Face recognition was designed for 200-person factories. It collects biometric data — triggering laws like Illinois' BIPA, which has produced settlements in the hundreds of millions — staff hate it, and a single bad scan in the morning rush is a 20-minute headache.
buddyPunchingTax.badGps: GPS / geofencing demands always-on location permission and drains batteries. It tells you a phone is near the shop — not whose phone, and not whether it's actually inside.
buddyPunchingTax.badOutro: Neither matches the budget or the ethics of a small café.
buddyPunchingTax.h2Fix: The actual fix: three small layers
buddyPunchingTax.fix1: The phone is bound to the person. First clock-in registers the device; later punches from someone else's phone don't count.
buddyPunchingTax.fix2: The clock-in is bound to your shop's network. Off-network punches are flagged or blocked. No GPS, no battery drain.
buddyPunchingTax.fix3: The punch is bound to your shop's NFC tag (beta). A coin-sized sticker by the espresso machine. The tap has to happen there.
buddyPunchingTax.fixOutro: To fake a punch you'd need a co-worker's enrolled phone, on your shop's WiFi, tapping a tag bolted to your wall. At that point they're just... at work.
buddyPunchingTax.todayCta: Read the <linkThreeFactor>three-factor explainer</linkThreeFactor> for the deeper conceptual story, see the <linkStopBuddy>stop buddy punching</linkStopBuddy> walk-through, or <linkSignup>start free</linkSignup> — up to 10 active employees, no credit card, no hardware.

newDeviceAlert.lead1: For most weeks, every staff phone is the same one as last week. Lyhour clocks in from her iPhone, Sokha from his Pixel. The pattern is so steady that when it breaks, the break is the signal.
newDeviceAlert.lead2: That's the new-device alert. The moment someone clocks in (or out) from a phone they've never used before on this account, DailyBrew pings the owner — push, Telegram, and now email in parallel, so it reaches you whether the app is open, you're in the kitchen, or you only check your inbox in the morning.
newDeviceAlert.h2Trigger: Why first-time-on-this-device is the right trigger
newDeviceAlert.triggerP1: GPS tells you a phone is near the shop. Face recognition tells you who's in front of the camera. Neither tells you whether the phone clocking in belongs to the person it's clocking in for.
newDeviceAlert.triggerP2: DailyBrew runs two device mechanisms in parallel. Device verification is the active one: within a single day, only one device per employee, and the check-out device has to match the check-in device. That blocks live buddy-punching attempts. The new-device alert — what this post is about — is the passive companion. It looks across the employee's full history of phones they've ever clocked in with, and pings you when one shows up that isn't in that history. It doesn't block; it just surfaces. That separation matters: the active check stops same-day fraud, the history check catches the slow drift (the staff member who quietly started using a different phone three weeks ago).
newDeviceAlert.triggerP3: One thing the alert is not: a ping every time someone clocks in. Subsequent uses of a phone we've already seen for that employee are silent — the message isn't "Lyhour clocked in", it's "Lyhour clocked in on a phone we've never seen before for her." If she upgrades her iPhone tomorrow, you get one alert; then the new iPhone joins her known-devices list and the next 200 punches from it are quiet. If you want every clock-in to ping you, that's a different feature — live check-in alerts — controlled by its own toggle.
newDeviceAlert.sample: ⚠️ New device used Lyhour Huy checked in from a new device. Device: iPhone 17 Pro Time: Apr 10, 09:00
newDeviceAlert.looksOutro: You get one push, one Telegram message, one email — for the same event. Not three separate decisions to make, just three places the same fact can reach you.
newDeviceAlert.h2When: When it's nothing — and when it isn't
newDeviceAlert.when1: Likely fine: the staff member upgraded phones over the weekend. New iPhone, same person. You reset their device binding in two taps (Console → Employees → Reset device) and the new phone becomes the baseline.
newDeviceAlert.when2: Worth a look: the alert lands during a shift you didn't expect anyone new to start. Or the device label reads "Android" for a barista who's been on iPhone for two years. Or the alert lands twice in one morning for two different people.
newDeviceAlert.when3: The buddy-punching tell: the new device is the same across two employees, two days in a row. That's the shared phone in the back room — and it's exactly the kind of pattern a normal time-clock report would hide.
newDeviceAlert.emailP1: Push notifications get muted. Telegram chats get archived. The morning-rush owner who's elbow-deep in milk steamers doesn't read either until the rush is over — and by then the alert is buried under a hundred other notifications.
newDeviceAlert.emailP2: Email is the durable record. You can search it weeks later, share it with a manager, forward it to your accountant if it turns into a wage dispute. The three channels aren't redundancy — they're three different time horizons.
newDeviceAlert.notThisP1: This isn't biometric. It isn't location tracking. It isn't a black-box "trust score." It's a single boolean: have we seen this device on this employee before? When the answer is no for the first time, we tell you. That's the whole feature.
newDeviceAlert.notThisP2: Available on Espresso and Double Espresso, on by default. No setting to enable — the moment a workspace has a paid plan and a baseline device on file, the detector starts watching.
newDeviceAlert.notThisCta: Read the <linkThreeFactor>three-factor explainer</linkThreeFactor> for the verification stack behind the alert, or <linkPricing>check pricing</linkPricing> to see what else Espresso unlocks.

liveCheckinAlerts.lead1: You can already get Telegram notifications for leave requests, shift changes, closures, and the daily summary. Now there's one more: live check-in/out pings, the moment they happen.
liveCheckinAlerts.lead2: It's off by default. Here's why — and why you might still want it on.
liveCheckinAlerts.offDefault1: A 5-person café with normal opening and closing shifts sees about 10 punches a day. Add lunch breaks (some workspaces clock those too) and you're at 20. That's 20 Telegram messages a day, just from one shop, on top of whatever else lives in your inbox.
liveCheckinAlerts.offDefault2: For most owners that's too much signal-shaped noise. The daily summary at 6pm tells you who showed up, who was late, who's still on a long shift — without 20 individual pings.
liveCheckinAlerts.offDefault3: But if you're not at the shop, or if you're scaling to a second location and want to feel the pulse of the place from across town, the live pings turn that into a steady, low-resolution sense of "yes, the morning crew is in" without staring at the dashboard.
liveCheckinAlerts.looksOutro: Two channels in parallel: your workspace Telegram group (if you've set one up under Settings → Telegram notifications), and your personal Telegram DM (if you've linked it on your profile). Same message, two destinations.
liveCheckinAlerts.step2: Make sure the master "Enable Telegram notifications" toggle is on (it gates the workspace group send)
liveCheckinAlerts.step3: Below the group chat setup, flip the new Live check-in/out alerts toggle
liveCheckinAlerts.howToOutro: That's the whole flow. Espresso plan and up — same plan tier as the rest of Telegram notifications.
liveCheckinAlerts.h2Pairs: How this pairs with other alerts
liveCheckinAlerts.pair1: Daily summary at 6pm (Telegram + email) — the digest that doesn't depend on this toggle
liveCheckinAlerts.pair2: New-device alert (Telegram + push + email) — fires when an employee uses a phone they've never used before, which is the alert you actually care about most of the time. Covered in the new-device alert post.
liveCheckinAlerts.pair3: Leave / shift / closure (Telegram + push + email) — administrative changes
liveCheckinAlerts.pairsOutro: The live check-in pings sit alongside these. Most owners turn them on for a week, get a feel for the rhythm of their shop, then either keep them or turn them off. There's no wrong answer.
liveCheckinAlerts.notThis: This isn't device verification. The toggle just decides whether you hear about each check-in — it doesn't change which check-ins are allowed. DailyBrew's device verification (a separate Espresso feature) enforces same-day consistency: within a single day, one device per employee, and the check-out has to come from the same device as the check-in. Tomorrow that same employee can use a different phone — no problem, the constraint is per-day, not per-lifetime. That keeps active buddy punching from succeeding silently. The live alerts let you watch it work.
liveCheckinAlerts.noise: If you turn this on and find yourself ignoring the pings, that's a signal to turn it off. Notifications you ignore train your brain to ignore the channel, which means the actually-urgent things (new-device alerts, leave requests) blend in. We left it off by default for that reason. Use it when the live feedback matters, mute it when it doesn't.
